1.1: 1-2 Chronicles was written to the post-exilic community (ca. 400-350 BCE) as a theological retelling of the monarchy. The Chronicler's perspective differs from Samuel-Kings: (1) he focuses almost exclusively on Judah and the Davidic line (the northern kingdom barely appears); (2) he omits many of David's failures (Bathsheba, Absalom) while including his worship and temple preparations; (3) he emphasizes the Levitical worship structure, the temple, and its proper celebration; (4) he ends on an upbeat note (Cyrus's decree, 2 Chr 36:22-23) rather than Kings' ambiguous ending (Jehoiachin's release). The Chronicler is writing a theology of hope for the restored community: YHWH's covenant with David is still in force; the temple worship is the proper center of life; the exile was judgment but not the end.
1.2: The Sethite line (vv1-3: Adam, Seth, Enosh, Kenan, Mahalalel, Jared) runs from Gen 5:1-20. The Chronicler strips away the ages and narratives, retaining only the names — the genealogy functions as a statement of continuity, not a biographical record. The line through Seth (rather than Cain) signals the covenant lineage: Seth was appointed as Abel’s replacement and from his son Enosh ‘people began to call on the name of YHWH’ (Gen 4:26).
1.3: Enoch, Methuselah, Lamech — the final pre-flood generation. Enoch is simply listed (v3); the Chronicler omits the narrative of his non-death (‘God took him,’ Gen 5:24), which spawned extensive Second Temple Jewish speculation about Enoch as heavenly scribe and apocalyptic seer (1 Enoch, Jubilees). The Chronicler’s genealogy is resolutely this-worldly: the names matter, not the legends surrounding them.
1.4: Noah and his three sons (Shem, Ham, Japheth) are the pivot of the genealogy: everything before is pre-flood prehistory; everything after represents the post-flood nations. The Table of Nations (Gen 10 = 1 Chr 1:5-23) is one of the most geographically comprehensive texts in the ancient world — a systematic mapping of known peoples and their ethnic relationships, composed in the early first millennium BCE and reflecting trade and diplomatic knowledge of the era.
1.5: The sons of Japheth represent the northern and western peoples from the Israelite geographic horizon. Gomer = the Cimmerians (Assyrian Gimirrai), a people from the Black Sea region who swept through Anatolia in the 7th century BCE — attested in Sargon II’s records. Magog = possibly the Scythians (Assyrian Ishkuzai), a steppe people; Ezekiel 38-39 uses Magog as the archetypal northern threat. Madai = the Medes, an Iranian people who co-destroyed Assyria (612 BCE). Javan = the Greeks/Ionians (Greek Iawones, Hebrew yāwān). Tubal and Meshech = Iron-Age peoples of Anatolia (Tabalian and Mushki kingdoms in modern Turkey), trading with Tyre in slaves and bronze (Ezek 27:13).
1.6: Ashkenaz = likely the Scythians (Assyrian Ishkuzai; in later Jewish tradition, Ashkenaz designated Germanic peoples, which is why European Jews are called Ashkenazim). Riphath (= Diphath in some manuscripts) — difficult to identify precisely; possibly Paphlagonians or Phrygians. Togarmah = the Hittite vassal kingdom of Tegarama (modern Gurun in Turkey); Ezekiel 27:14 notes Togarmah traded horses and mules with Tyre; Ezek 38:6 includes Togarmah in the coalition against Gog.
1.7: Elishah = possibly Cyprus (Alashiya, attested in Egyptian and Ugaritic records as a major copper-exporting island; Ezek 27:7 notes linen from Elishah for Tyre). Tarshish = a distant western trading port, usually identified with Tartessus in Spain (or possibly Sardinia); Jonah flees there as the farthest point from Israel’s coast. Kittim = Cyprus/Aegean peoples; in later texts it shifts to mean Rome (Dan 11:30; 1 Macc 1:1). Dodanim (= Rodanim in Gen 10:4) = Rhodes (Rodos).
1.8: The sons of Ham represent African and Canaanite peoples. Cush = upper Nile Africa (classical Ethiopia/Nubia; modern Sudan); the Cushite kingdom of Kush (ancient Meroe) was a major power. Egypt = Miṣrayim (a dual form, possibly North + South Egypt). Put = Libya (North African coast west of Egypt; attested in Egyptian records as Puṭ). Canaan = the pre-Israelite inhabitants of the land — their listing under Ham establishes the theological basis for Israelite displacement: Canaanites are not in the Shemite/Abrahamic covenant line.
1.9: The sons of Cush include Seba (upper Nile region), Havilah (gold-bearing land, Gen 2:11; possibly Arabia), Raamah, and the trade-pair Sheba and Dedan (South Arabian kingdoms, major incense-route traders; the Queen of Sheba came from this region, 2 Chr 9:1). The sons of Raamah (Sheba and Dedan) are listed separately, demonstrating the Table of Nations’ interest in tracing trading networks as much as ethnic groups.
1.10: Nimrod is the Table of Nations’ only narrative interruption: he became the ‘first mighty warrior (gibbôr) on earth,’ founder of Mesopotamian cities (Gen 10:8-12 adds: Babel, Erech, Accad, Calneh in Shinar, then Nineveh, Calah, and others). In the ANE tradition, Mesopotamian kingship was the paradigm of earthly power — the list of cities reads like the Sumerian King List’s urban centers. Nimrod has been identified variously with Gilgamesh, Naram-Sin, Tukulti-Ninurta I, and others, but no certain identification holds. He represents the founding of the imperial Mesopotamian political tradition that would eventually exile Israel.
1.11: Egypt’s sons are regions or sub-groups of the Nile Valley: Ludim (possibly Libyan mercenaries in Egyptian service), Anamim, Lehabim (= Libyans?), Naphtuhim (possibly the Delta region or northern Egypt — npt = Delta in Egyptian). These are ethnic-geographic designations for peoples within Egypt’s cultural sphere rather than named nations with capital cities.
1.12: Pathrusim = Upper Egypt (Paṯrōs in Hebrew, from Egyptian p3 t3 rsy, ‘the southland’; Isa 11:11 and Jer 44:1 locate the Jewish diaspora there). Casluhim — obscure; the Table of Nations identifies them as the ancestors of the Philistines. This is the most politically significant genealogical note in this section: the Philistines, Israel’s most persistent enemy in the period of the judges and early monarchy, originated from Egypt’s broader cultural sphere. Caphtorim = the people of Caphtor (= Crete or the Aegean; Deut 2:23 and Amos 9:7 also connect the Philistines to Caphtor).
1.13: Canaan’s firstborn is Sidon — the Phoenician city-state that would become one of Israel’s most important trading partners and neighbors. Heth = the Hittites; in the patriarchal narratives, the Hittites (Heth) are Canaanite residents of Hebron (Gen 23). These are distinct from the Anatolian Hittite Empire — probably related descendants or a southern branch. The Hittite Neo-Hittite city-states (Carchemish, Hamath) persisted into the 9th-8th centuries BCE as Aramaic-speaking states.
1.14: The Jebusites = pre-Israelite inhabitants of Jerusalem (Jebus); David captured the city from them (2 Sam 5:6-7). Amorites = a broad category for Canaanite highland peoples; in cuneiform, Amurru (Westerners) was a general Mesopotamian term for the peoples west of the Euphrates. Girgashites = mentioned only in the canonical lists of Canaanite nations; no extra-biblical attestation.
1.15: Hivites = possibly related to the Hurrians (known as Horites in the OT); the Hivites of Shechem (Gen 34) and Gibeon (Josh 9) were Canaanite inhabitants who negotiated with the patriarchs and Israel. Arkites and Sinites — city-based groups along the Phoenician coast; Arqa (modern Tell Arqa in Lebanon) is attested in Egyptian topographical lists. Arvadites = Arvad (modern Ruad, an island city off the Syrian coast; Ezek 27:8,11 records Arvad’s maritime role).
1.16: Zemarites = Simyra, a Phoenician coastal city (attested in the Amarna letters as Sumur). Hamathites = the people of Hamath (modern Hama on the Orontes River in Syria), a major Aramaic kingdom in the early Iron Age; ‘the entrance of Hamath’ marked Israel’s northern boundary in ideal maps (Num 34:8; 1 Kgs 8:65). These final Canaanite peoples complete the list of nations that Israelite memory associated with the pre-conquest population of the land.
1.17: The sons of Shem represent the Semitic peoples. Elam = the ancient civilization of southwestern Iran (Khuzestan), a rival to Mesopotamian powers and a major actor in the patriarchal period (Gen 14:1: Chedorlaomer king of Elam). Asshur = Assyria (the Assyrian heartland around the upper Tigris). Arphaxad = ancestor of Abraham (vv18,24-27); the name may relate to the Chaldean/Chaldaean region. Aram = the Arameans, centered in Syria (Damascus); the Hebrew of the OT is closely related to Aramaic, and the patriarchs have Aramean family connections (Gen 28:5; Deut 26:5: ‘a wandering Aramean was my father’).
1.18: The Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber chain is the trunk line leading to Abraham. Eber is the eponymous ancestor of the ʿiḇriyyîm (Hebrews; Gen 10:21 calls Shem ‘the father of all the sons of Eber’). The line through Eber represents the covenant lineage narrowing: from all Semitic peoples through Eber to Abraham to Israel. The name ‘Hebrew’ connects the people to this genealogical ancestor.
1.19: Peleg = the line that leads to Abraham (v25); his name means ‘division’ (pālg in Ugaritic; Hebrew pālag, to divide). The text explains: ‘in his time the earth was divided.’ The traditional interpretation connects this to the Tower of Babel (Gen 11:1-9): the division of languages scattered humanity. Some scholars propose a geographic division (tectonic, irrigation, or political). The Babel narrative (Gen 11) likely follows chronologically at the Peleg generation, which the Chronicler omits but assumes. Joktan = the other branch from Eber, leading to South Arabian peoples (vv20-23).
1.20: The sons of Joktan (vv20-23) represent South Arabian peoples — the tribal geography of the Arabian Peninsula as known from Israelite trade contacts. Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth (= Hadramawt, the major South Arabian kingdom in classical antiquity, modern Hadramout in Yemen), Jerah (possibly a lunar-cult center, yaraḥ = moon).
1.21: Hadoram, Uzal (= Sanaa, the capital of Yemen in classical Arabic sources?), Diklah (possibly the date-palm region of the Arabian Peninsula — diqla = date palm in Aramaic). These South Arabian identifications align with what is known of pre-Islamic Arabian tribal geography and are partially confirmed by South Arabian inscriptions.
1.22: Ebal, Abimael, Sheba (the most famous South Arabian kingdom, center of the incense trade; the Queen of Sheba who visited Solomon represents this wealthy polity). The Sheba here in the Joktanite list (South Arabian) is distinct from the son of Raamah in the Hamite line (v9) — the Table of Nations reflects the complexity of Arabian peoples’ origins, distributing ‘Sheba’ traditions across two lineages.
1.23: Ophir = the legendary gold source of Solomon’s fleet (1 Kgs 9:28; 10:11); probable location in South Arabia or East Africa (Somalia/Punt); some scholars propose India. The exact identification is disputed. Havilah (also a Cushite son in v9) and Jobab complete the Joktanite list. The summary ‘all these were sons of Joktan’ closes the South Arabian section and redirects attention to the line that leads to Abraham (v24).
1.24: The Chronicler now compresses the full Shem-to-Abraham genealogy of Gen 11:10-26 into a bare name-list (vv24-27), stripping away the ages that Gen 11 preserves. The ages (Shem lived 500 years after the flood; Arphaxad lived 403 years after Shelah was born; etc.) were of interest to the Gen 11 author for establishing the chronological framework; the Chronicler’s interest is the covenant lineage, not the chronology.
1.25: Eber, Peleg, Reu — the middle section of the Shem-to-Abraham chain. Peleg is already explained at v19 (earth divided); Reu is otherwise unknown. The Chronicler passes through these names at speed — the genealogical purpose is to establish the unbroken line from Shem (the blessed son of Noah, Gen 9:26) to Abraham without any gap.
1.26: Serug, Nahor, Terah — the final three links before Abraham. Nahor is Abraham’s grandfather (and also the name of Abraham’s brother, Gen 11:26-29). Terah = Abraham’s father, who began the journey from Ur of the Chaldeans toward Canaan but died in Haran (Gen 11:31-32). The city of Haran (modern Harran in southeastern Turkey) is archaeologically attested as a major Mesopotamian city and a center of moon-god worship.
1.27: Abram, that is, Abraham — the Chronicler uses both names without explanation: ʾaḇrām hûʾ ʾaḇrāhām. The parenthetical gloss (‘that is, Abraham’) acknowledges the name-change in Gen 17:5 without retelling the covenant-renewal narrative behind it. The arrival at Abraham ends the universal history section (vv1-27) and begins the particular covenant history — the narrowing from all humanity to the one man through whom ‘all nations would be blessed’ (Gen 12:3).
1.28: Abraham’s two covenantal sons are listed together: Isaac (the son of promise through Sarah) and Ishmael (the son of Hagar). The Chronicler places them on equal formal footing in the genealogical list before separating their lines (vv29-33 = Ishmael; v34 = Isaac). The equal listing in v28 reflects the genealogical fact — both are Abraham’s sons — without resolving the theological distinction that the narrative tradition maintains.
1.29: The Ishmaelite genealogy (vv29-31) traces twelve tribes, paralleling the twelve tribes of Israel — a structural deliberateness that signals Ishmael’s status as a ‘great nation’ promised to him (Gen 17:20). Nebaioth = oldest brother; traditionally identified with the Nabataeans, the kingdom that controlled the incense routes from Arabia to the Mediterranean (capital: Petra; flourished 4th century BCE – 2nd century CE). Kedar = major North Arabian tribe, prominent in prophetic texts as desert-dwellers (Isa 60:7; Jer 2:10; Ezek 27:21).
1.30: Mishma, Dumah, Massa — Dumah is identified with an oasis in northern Arabia (mentioned in Isa 21:11; Assyrian records: Adumatu); Massa appears in Prov 30:1 and 31:1 as the homeland of wisdom writers Agur and Lemuel, suggesting a North Arabian wisdom tradition. Hadad is a name borne by Edomite kings as well (v51), reflecting shared divine names across the Aramean-Arabian cultural sphere (Hadad = the storm god).
1.31: Jetur, Naphish, and Kedemah complete the twelve Ishmaelite tribes. Jetur and Naphish are the tribes against whom the Transjordanian Israelites fought (1 Chr 5:19), associating these Ishmaelites with the Syrian desert fringe. The summary ‘These were the sons of Ishmael’ closes the Ishmaelite section; the genealogy then shifts to the Keturah-line (v32), the ‘lesser’ Abrahamic descendants through his concubine.
1.32: The sons of Keturah, Abraham’s concubine (Gen 25:1-4): six sons representing Arabian and Midianite tribes. Zimran and Jokshan are otherwise unknown. Medan = possibly near Midian. Midian = the most significant of the six — the Midianites are central to the Moses and Exodus narratives (Jethro, Moses’s father-in-law, is a Midianite priest); they also appear as caravan traders who carried Joseph to Egypt (Gen 37:28) and as Israel’s opponents in Judges (Gideon, Judg 6-8). Ishbak and Shuah (Shuah = Job’s friend Bildad the Shuhite, Job 2:11 — placing Job’s dialogue partners in this Arabian-adjacent cultural zone).
1.33: The sons of Midian include Ephah (mentioned in Isa 60:6 as bringing camels and gold from Midian to Jerusalem at the eschatological ingathering) and Epher, Hanoch, Abida, Eldaah. These represent the tribal sub-groups of Midian in the Transjordanian and North Arabian steppe. The summary closes the Keturah section; Abraham’s other descendants (through Hagar and Keturah) are located in the Arabian-steppe zone — the surrounding nations against whose backdrop Israel’s covenant identity is defined.
1.34: The pivot: ‘Abraham fathered Isaac. The sons of Isaac: Esau and Israel.’ The parallelism is exact — first Abraham and his two sons, now Isaac and his two sons. Esau = Edom (the name-equation is stated in Gen 36:1,8). Israel = Jacob, whose name-change (Gen 32:28; 35:10) the Chronicler again notes through the covenant name only, without the narrative. The selection of ‘Israel’ rather than ‘Jacob’ signals the Chronicler’s focus: what matters is the covenant identity, not the biographical individual.
1.35: The sons of Esau (vv35-37) parallel the structure of the Ishmaelite and Keturahite lists. Esau’s sons through his Canaanite and Horite wives: Eliphaz (the most significant — through him Amalek; and Eliphaz is the name of Job’s most prominent friend, Job 2:11, from Teman, an Edomite region). Reuel, Jeush, Jalam, Korah. The Edomite genealogy prepares for the king list in vv43-54.
1.36: The sons of Eliphaz include Teman — the most famous Edomite clan/region, renowned for wisdom (Jer 49:7: ‘Is there no wisdom in Teman? Has counsel perished from the prudent?’; Obad 1:8-9). Amalek = the Amalekites; his mother is Timna (v36; Gen 36:12: Timna was Eliphaz’s concubine), connecting Amalek to both the Edomite and Horite lines. The Amalekites are Israel’s first attacker in the wilderness (Exod 17:8-16) and the object of YHWH’s perpetual war (Exod 17:16).
1.37: The sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, Mizzah — these four names reappear as Edomite chiefs in vv37 and 51-54 (Nahath = chief Aliah?), reflecting the clan-based leadership structure of Edom. The Edomite social structure was organized around clans (ʾallûpîm, chiefs) rather than tribes in the Israelite sense.
1.38: The sons of Seir (vv38-42) — the Horites, the pre-Edomite inhabitants of the land of Edom (the region south of the Dead Sea). Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, Dishan. The Horites (traditionally identified with the Hurrians, a people of northern Mesopotamian origin who settled widely in the 2nd millennium BCE) were the indigenous population before Esau’s descendants displaced and intermarried with them (Gen 36:20-30). The genealogical inclusion of both Edomite and Horite lines reflects the historical intermingling of these populations.
1.39: The sons of Lotan: Hori and Homam. Timna was Lotan’s sister — the same Timna who became the concubine of Eliphaz (v36), uniting the Edomite and Horite lines through her. This cross-reference is the genealogy’s subtlest note on Edomite-Horite integration: the concubine who bore Amalek is herself a Horite woman, making Amalek genetically Horite as well as Edomite.
1.40: Shobal’s sons include Alian, Manahath, Ebal — Manahath is also the name of a Benjaminite place of exile (1 Chr 8:6) and a clan within Judah (2:52,54), suggesting that Edomite/Horite names infiltrated the tribal territories through later settlement patterns. Zibeon’s sons: Aiah and Anah — the same Anah who discovered hot springs in the wilderness (Gen 36:24; LXX has ‘water’, MT has ‘mules’/‘springs’), a detail about Anah’s character that the genealogy preserves.
1.41: Anah’s son Dishon (distinguishable from the Dishon of v38, Seir’s son). Dishon’s sons: Hamran, Eshban, Ithran, Cheran — four Horite clan-names otherwise unattested outside the genealogical tradition. These represent the sub-tribal structure of the Horite/Seir population.
1.42: The sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, Jaakan (yaʿăqān — same root as Jacob?). Bilhan is also a Benjaminite name (1 Chr 7:10). The sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran. Uz = the land of Job (Job 1:1); the geographic association of Uz with the Edomite/Horite region places the Job narrative in the south Transjordanian zone, consistent with the book’s setting among non-Israelite sages with Edomite connections (Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite).
1.43: The Edomite king list (vv43-54 = Gen 36:31-43) is introduced with a historically significant phrase: ‘these are the kings who reigned in Edom before any king reigned over Israel.’ The Edomites had a monarchy before Israel — the list predates Saul (ca. 1020 BCE). The kings in the list are non-dynastic: each comes from a different clan, with no succession from father to son. Bela son of Beor (whose city was Dinhabah) — the only element of this list that has attracted modern comparison is ‘son of Beor,’ which is also Balaam’s patronymic (Num 22:5); the connection is almost certainly coincidental.
1.44: Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah — Bozrah (modern Buseirah, Jordan) was one of the most important Edomite cities, the likely administrative center of the Edomite kingdom in the Iron Age. Excavations at Buseirah have revealed a major Iron Age fortress and administrative complex (ca. 8th-7th century BCE). Bozrah is mentioned prominently in prophetic judgments against Edom (Isa 34:6; 63:1; Jer 49:13,22; Amos 1:12), indicating its status as Edom’s chief city.
1.45: Husham of the land of the Temanites — Teman was the most famous Edomite district, renowned for wisdom (Jer 49:7; Obad 1:8). Husham’s origin in Teman places a third of the early Edomite kings in the southern region of Edom (around the Wadi Arabah). The land of Teman = the northern Edomite plateau; Temanite wisdom is attested through Eliphaz the Temanite (Job 2:11) as the preeminent friend of Job.
1.46: Hadad son of Bedad, who ‘defeated Midian on the territory of Moab’ — this is the only king in the list with a recorded military action. The battle against Midian in Moabite territory is unattested elsewhere but fits a period of Midianite-Moabite conflict in the region. Hadad is a storm-god name widespread in the Aramean-Edomite cultural zone (the Aramean god Hadad = Baal; Edomite naming the king Hadad). His city was Avith.
1.47: Samlah of Masrekah — location uncertain (perhaps in northern Edom); no recorded action. The succession formula is consistent: each king succeeds the previous one without relationship (wayyimlōk taḥtāyw, ‘and he reigned in his place’). This non-dynastic succession pattern distinguishes Edom from the later Davidic dynasty, where hereditary succession was the Chronicler’s ideal.
1.48: Shaul of Rehoboth-by-the-River — ‘the River’ most likely = the Euphrates (as in most OT usage), which would make Rehoboth-by-the-River a city in northern Syria; alternatively, the Wadi Zered (a valley in Moab/Edom) has been proposed. This king may represent a period of Edomite territorial expansion northward, or the name may reflect a different river entirely. The Shaul/Saul correspondence with Israel’s first king is noted by some commentators but may be coincidental.
1.49: Baal-hanan son of Achbor — the only compound Baal-name in the Edomite king list, indicating that Baal worship was present in the royal naming convention. Achbor is also the name of Josiah’s secretary (2 Kgs 22:12,14), suggesting the name was current in later periods as well. Baal-hanan’s reign follows the standard succession formula without military note.
1.50: Hadad — a second king named Hadad (as in v46; the duplication reflects common Edomite royal naming). His city was Pai (= Pau in Gen 36:39). His wife was Mehetabel daughter of Matred daughter of Me-zahab — the only queen mentioned in the list, and uniquely with a two-generation matrilineal genealogy. This detail is preserved from the original Edomite court records and reflects the importance of the queen mother’s lineage in the Near Eastern court tradition (cf. the gᵉḇîrāh institution in Judah).
1.51: ‘Hadad also died’ — this phrase (the last king’s death without a named successor) suggests the king list was compiled at a moment when dynastic succession had broken down or the list had reached its source’s end. The transition from kings to chiefs (ʾallûpîm) (vv51-54) may reflect a later period when Edom was organized tribally rather than monarchically, or a different source tradition. The chiefs may have been contemporaneous with the kings rather than successive.
1.52: Chiefs Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon — Oholibamah is also the name of one of Esau’s wives (Gen 36:2,5,14), a Horite woman; the chief’s name matching a wife’s name suggests clan naming after eponymous ancestors. Pinon = possibly Feinan (ancient Punon), a major copper-smelting center in the Arabah valley south of the Dead Sea, attested in Egyptian records as a source of copper and location of Israelite stops in the wilderness (Num 33:42-43: Punon).
1.53: Chiefs Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar — Kenaz reappears as a clan within Judah: Caleb’s father was Jephunneh the Kenizzite (Num 32:12); Othniel son of Kenaz was the first judge of Israel (Judg 3:9). The incorporation of a Kenizzite (Edomite-adjacent) clan into Judah is a historical example of the ethnic fluidity at the margins of Israelite identity. Teman appears again as a chief, confirming the region’s importance.
1.54: Chiefs Magdiel and Iram complete the list. The closing formula ‘These were the chiefs of Edom’ ends chapter 1. The Chronicler’s decision to include the Edomite genealogy and king list in full — despite the Edomite-Israelite enmity — reflects the genealogical comprehensiveness of the Table of Nations framework: all nations are traced to their origins, establishing YHWH’s sovereignty over all peoples, not only Israel. The Edomite list also implicitly sets up the subsequent Davidic monarchy as the fulfillment of Israel’s own delayed kingship — the nations had kings first, but Israel’s king would ultimately be greater.

2.4: The Chronicler’s inclusion of Perez and Zerah as sons of Tamar and Judah (Gen 38) in the Judahite genealogy is significant because it embeds the royal line’s most irregular origin in the genealogy without comment. Perez (pereṣ = ‘breach’) is the ancestor of the Davidic line (2:5 → 2:9-17 → David); the line runs through an act of levirate deception. Gen 38’s social-legal context involves the levirate custom (yibbum) by which a surviving brother was obligated to marry a deceased brother’s widow to raise up offspring for him (Deut 25:5-10). Judah’s failure to give his son Shelah to Tamar (Gen 38:11, 14) and Tamar’s initiative to secure her levirate rights through disguised intercourse with Judah are the background. The Chronicler does not retell this — he simply records the names. Ancient genealogies regularly included irregular origins: the Sumerian King List includes kings whose origins involved divine-human relations; the Egyptian royal genealogies occasionally suppress irregular successions. The Chronicler’s matter-of-fact inclusion of Tamar’s sons reflects the genealogy’s purpose: to establish covenant-lineage continuity, not to edit the moral complexity of the ancestors. Matt 1:3 follows the same practice, naming Tamar among the four women in Jesus’s genealogy, all of whom have irregular situations.
2.10: The genealogical sequence from Ram to Jesse (2:10-12) — Ram, Amminadab, Nahshon, Salmon, Boaz, Obed, Jesse — is the same sequence preserved in Ruth 4:18-22 and reproduced in Matt 1:3-6. The convergence of these three genealogical lists (Chronicles, Ruth, Matthew) on the same ten-generation sequence from Perez to David indicates the stability of this particular lineage tradition in post-exilic Jewish memory. The appearance of Nahshon son of Amminadab is significant: Nahshon was the leader of the tribe of Judah during the wilderness period (Num 1:7; 2:3; 7:12) and is connected to the first tribal offering at the tabernacle’s dedication (Num 7:12-17). The genealogy thus links the Davidic line to the wilderness-era tribal leadership and to Aaron (Amminadab’s daughter Elisheba married Aaron, Exod 6:23) — providing both royal and priestly genealogical connections for the Davidic lineage. This breadth of connection — Davidic king and Aaronide priest simultaneously connected through Amminadab — is the kind of genealogical significance the post-exilic community, which lacked a king but retained a high priest, would have found theologically resonant.
2.1: The Chronicler opens Israel's story not with the Exodus but with genealogy, anchoring post-exilic community identity in covenant lineage. The twelve sons of Israel (Jacob) provide the canonical frame for all that follows. Writing for Diaspora returnees who needed to know *who* they were before asking *where* they belonged, the Chronicler traces every tribe back to these twelve names.
2.2: Dan is listed second among the twelve, an unusual position reflecting geographical arrangement rather than birth order. The Chronicler's inclusion of all twelve — including Dan, often omitted in prophetic contexts — signals his intent to address the whole restored community, not just Judah and Levi.
2.3: Judah's three eldest sons — Er, Onan, and Shelah — were born of Bathshua, a Canaanite woman. The Chronicler notes Er's death by YHWH's judgment without further elaboration (cf. Gen 38:7). That the chosen tribe's line begins through a Canaanite mother reflects the Chronicler's honest acknowledgment of Israel's mixed origins while insisting that YHWH's election persists regardless.
2.5: Perez and Zerah, twin sons of Tamar, launch two distinct branches of Judah's line. The Perez branch will lead through Hezron to David; the Zerah branch will be noted for its famous sages (v6). That the Davidic line descends from Perez — born of a levirate-adjacent union — underscores that divine election operates through unlikely human circumstances.
2.6: The five sons of Zerah include Ethan, Heman, Calcol, and Dara — the same legendary sages to whom Psalm titles are attributed (cf. 1 Kgs 4:31 where Solomon's wisdom surpasses them). The Chronicler places these cultural heroes within Judah's genealogy, claiming Israel's wisdom tradition as a Judahite inheritance, not borrowed from Canaan.
2.7: Zimri's son Achan is renamed "Achar" (troubler) — a deliberate pun encoding theological judgment. The reference to the ban (ḥērem) he violated at Jericho (Josh 7) reminds post-exilic readers that covenant faithfulness determines the community's success. The name-change is the Chronicler's only comment; it functions as a permanent memorial of the consequence of treachery against YHWH.
2.8: Ethan son of Zerah is likely the Ethan the Ezrahite of Psalm 89, whose maskil meditates on YHWH's faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. Placing his genealogical root here in Judah gives that psalm a Judahite context, linking wisdom poetry with covenant promise in the Chronicler's literary vision.
2.9: Hezron's three sons — Jerahmeel, Ram, and Chelubai (Caleb) — launch three major genealogical branches that occupy most of chapter 2. Ram's line leads directly to David (vv10–17); Caleb's line encompasses the central Judahite clans who settled Bethlehem and Hebron; Jerahmeel's line preserves southern Negev clans. Together they map the human geography of the tribe of Judah across its historical territory.
2.11: Ram → Amminadab → Nahshon: this three-generation sequence bridges Israel's Egyptian sojourn to the wilderness period. Nahshon son of Amminadab led the tribe of Judah in the wilderness census (Num 2:3) and marched at the head of the camp (Num 10:14). His position as first among the tribal leaders foreshadows Judah's eventual supremacy and the Davidic monarchy.
2.12: Salma (Salmon) son of Nahshon is the father of Boaz, husband of Ruth the Moabite. The genealogy moves at speed through four names — Nahshon/Salma/Boaz/Obed — that the book of Ruth expanded into a full narrative. For the Chronicler, the inclusion of Ruth's line (without naming her) affirms that the Moabite woman's loyalty to Naomi placed her fully within the covenant genealogy leading to David.
2.13: Jesse, son of Obed, is named simply as father of seven sons, with David as the seventh. The Chronicler lists all seven brothers before naming David — the same literary inversion that the Samuel narrative used when the LORD rejected the elder brothers before anointing the youngest. The genealogy enshrines the memory that David's elevation was divine selection, not birth order.
2.14: Jesse's sons listed by birth order — Eliab/Abinadab/Shimea/Nethanel/Raddai/Ozem/David — differ slightly from 1 Sam 16–17 names, reflecting variant traditions or different names for the same individuals. The Chronicler's interest is not the variance but the lineage: every name is a stone in the foundation of the Davidic house.
2.15: David is identified as the seventh son, though 1 Sam 17:12 implies eight sons. The discrepancy may reflect that one son died without issue and was omitted from genealogical records, or that "seventh" carries symbolic weight (completeness/divine favor) that the Chronicler deliberately highlights. In any case, David's position as last-named and least-expected mirrors his narrative arc from shepherd to king.
2.16: David's two sisters — Zeruiah and Abigail — are named with their sons: Joab, Abishai, and Asahel (sons of Zeruiah), and Amasa (son of Abigail by Jether the Ishmaelite/Israelite). Women are rare in Chronicles genealogies; their inclusion here reflects the military importance of Zeruiah's sons as David's generals. Joab in particular will appear in multiple Chronicles narratives as the commander-in-chief whose career spans David's entire reign.
2.17: Amasa son of Jether the Ishmaelite — called "Israelite" in 2 Sam 17:25 — was Absalom's general in the rebellion, later reconciled to David (2 Sam 19:13), then killed by Joab (2 Sam 20:10). The Chronicler lists him without narrative comment, preserving the genealogical record while leaving readers who knew the Samuel narrative to supply the tragic context.
2.18: Caleb son of Hezron (distinct from Caleb son of Jephunneh the spy) is introduced with his two wives Azubah and Jerioth, then his concubine Ephrath. This Calebite line anchors the Bethlehem-Ephrathah tradition — the geographic heartland of David's family. Hur, son of Caleb and Ephrath, becomes the grandfather of Bezalel the tabernacle craftsman (v20), grounding Israel's sacred architecture in Judah's clan history.
2.19: Caleb's wife Ephrath bore him Hur — the same Hur who stood beside Aaron to support Moses' arms during the battle with Amalek (Exod 17:12). The Chronicler weaves together the lineages of Israel's warriors, craftsmen, and leaders into a continuous tapestry, showing that each generation's faithfulness prepared the ground for the next.
2.20: Hur's son Uri, and Uri's son Bezalel: the master craftsman who built the tabernacle in the wilderness (Exod 31:1–6; 35:30–36:2) traces his lineage directly through the Davidic-ancestral clan. YHWH's selection of Bezalel — filled with divine Spirit, wisdom, and skill — thus runs through the same bloodline that would produce the temple's patron king. The tabernacle and the temple share genealogical roots.
2.21: Hezron's later marriage to the daughter of Machir (father of Gilead), when he was sixty years old, produced Segub, father of Jair. This union explains the Israelite presence in Gilead: twenty-three cities of Jair in the region of Gilead (Num 32:41; Judg 10:3) trace back to this marriage between Hezron of Judah and a Gileadite princess, blending Judah and Manasseh's territorial claims.
2.22: Jair controlled twenty-three cities in Gilead before Geshur and Aram captured them. The reference to "Kenath and its villages, sixty cities" reflects the conquest of northern Transjordanian territory under Moses/Joshua. The Chronicler preserves these territorial memories because post-exilic returnees needed to understand the historical basis for Israelite land claims in the broader region.
2.23: Geshur and Aram taking the towns of Jair — along with Kenath and sixty villages — represents Israel's failure to hold the Transjordanian fringe. The Chronicler includes these losses without theological commentary, simply noting they belonged to the sons of Machir father of Gilead. The genealogy is also a map of contested territory.
2.24: After Hezron's death at Caleb-ephrathah, his widow Abijah bore him Ashhur posthumously (or Caleb inherited her). Ashhur as "father of Tekoa" plants the genealogical roots of the town that would later produce the prophet Amos (Amos 1:1). The Chronicler's genealogies thus embed prophetic geography within clan history.
2.25: Jerahmeel, Hezron's firstborn, had five sons by his first wife Atarah ("crown"), with Onam as the primary continuing line. The Jerahmeel clans occupied the Negev south of Hebron and are mentioned in 1 Sam 27:10 as an area David raided while at Ziklag — suggesting these were frontier clans with complex loyalties, now fully incorporated into the restored Judahite genealogy.
2.26: Jerahmeel's second wife Atarah bore Onam. Two wives generating parallel genealogical lines is a common Chronicles pattern; it reflects the polygynous household structures of the period while also preserving distinct clan traditions that might otherwise have been lost in the consolidation of post-exilic Judah.
2.27: Ram son of Jerahmeel — not the same Ram as David's ancestor (v9) — had sons Maaz, Jamin, and Eker. The multiplication of "Ram" names within Judah's genealogy reflects common naming patterns where important ancestral names recurred across clan branches. The Chronicler preserves both lines without conflating them.
2.28: Onam's sons Shammai and Jada continue the Jerahmeel line. Shammai's son Nadab leads to the next genealogical branch. These relatively obscure Jerahmeel names anchor clans that had historical territories in the southern Judahite Negev — regions that the returned exiles would need to establish legal claim to in Persian-period Yehud.
2.29: Abishur's wife Abihail bore sons Ahban and Molid. Female names appear in Chronicles genealogies when women were foundational to a clan's identity — either as sole bearers of the line or as women of significant social standing whose names were preserved in clan memory. "Abihail" ("father of strength") and similar names reflect the honorific naming culture of Judahite families.
2.30: Nadab's sons Seled and Appaim: Seled died without sons, while Appaim continued the line. The note that Seled died childless is genealogically important — it explains why that branch ends rather than continuing. The Chronicler's genealogies frequently note extinctions of lines alongside continuations, modeling the fragility of human lineages against the persistence of YHWH's covenant purposes.
2.31: Ishi son of Appaim; Sheshan son of Ishi; Ahlai from Sheshan: at v34 the Chronicler will note that Sheshan had no sons, only daughters, which leads to a daughter-in-law marriage arrangement. The genealogy is already tracking toward this remarkable detail that will explain how an Egyptian slave Jarha came to carry on a Judahite clan line.
2.32: Jada's sons Jether and Jonathan: Jether died without sons; Jonathan continued the line. These parallel endings — one branch stopping, one continuing — reflect the demographic reality of ancient families: infant mortality, warfare, disease, and childlessness constantly threatened genealogical continuity, making every surviving line feel like a preserved gift.
2.33: Jonathan's sons Peleth and Zaza close the Jerahmeel line as the Chronicler traces it. The phrase "these were the sons of Jerahmeel" frames the entire section as a completed genealogical unit — a formal closure that the Chronicler uses throughout chapters 2–8 to mark the transition from one clan's history to the next.
2.34: Sheshan's absence of male heirs forces an unusual solution: he gives his daughter in marriage to Jarha, his Egyptian servant/slave. This is one of the most striking passages in Chronicles genealogies — an Egyptian slave entering the covenant people's genealogical record through marriage. The Chronicler preserves this without embarrassment, consistent with his inclusive genealogical vision where even foreigners can be absorbed into Israel's story through covenant relationship.
2.35: Jarha the Egyptian married Sheshan's daughter, who bore Attai. The continuing line — Attai → Nathan → Zabad (v36) → Ephlal → Obed → Jehu → Azariah → Helez → Eleasah → Sismai → Shallum → Jekamiah → Elishama — runs for thirteen generations from Jarha. An Egyptian's descendants are listed as Judahite clansmen thirteen generations on. The genealogy enacts what the Chronicler believed: Israel's identity was covenantal and adoptable, not purely ethnic.
2.36: Nathan son of Attai → Zabad: the line continues unbroken through Jarha's descendants. The Chronicler gives this Egyptian-origin line the same genealogical dignity as the purely Israelite lines — same format, same number of generations, same formal closure. Post-exilic readers who were themselves of mixed ancestry may have found hope in this precedent.
2.37: Zabad → Ephlal → Obed: three generations passing the clan memory forward. The names here — particularly Zabad ("gift/endowed") — reflect the religious naming culture that affirmed YHWH's providential blessing even within a genealogical line of mixed origin. Every generation re-narrates the clan's identity through the names given to children.
2.38: Obed → Jehu: the name Jehu appears multiple times in Chronicles (the prophet Jehu son of Hanani, the king of Israel) — reflecting common naming patterns. This genealogical Jehu occupies a different branch entirely, demonstrating how the Chronicler distinguishes individuals with identical names through genealogical context rather than narrative description.
2.39: Jehu → Azariah → Helez: continuing the thirteen-generation descent from Jarha. By this point the line has been fully Israelite for many generations — the Egyptian origin is genealogical memory only. The Chronicler preserves it because honest genealogy serves communal identity: knowing where you came from, even when it includes foreigners, is part of knowing who you are.
2.40: Helez → Eleasah → Sismai: the names become less well-known as the genealogy moves further from its famous anchor points. This is characteristic of oral genealogical tradition: the first and last names are most clearly remembered (linked to known figures or living descendants), while middle generations are often preserved only as a chain of names.
2.41: Sismai → Shallum → Jekamiah → Elishama: these final four generations bring Jarha's line to approximately the time of David, suggesting this genealogy was compiled as a contemporary document rather than reconstructed from distant memory. Elishama as a contemporary clan head in David's era would have appeared in administrative records, anchoring the list's final entry in a verifiable moment.
2.42: Caleb son of Hezron (the third son of Hezron, v9) returns for a second genealogical section. His firstborn Mesha is the "father of Ziph" — the wilderness region where David hid from Saul (1 Sam 23:14). The Calebite roots of Ziph explain why the Ziphites could betray David (1 Sam 23:19): they were his own clan cousins who sided with Saul. Geography and genealogy are inseparable in Chronicles.
2.43: Mareshite towns and "Hebron" settlements trace back through Caleb's line. Hebron — the city where Abraham buried Sarah, where David was first crowned, where the patriarchal covenant was anchored — is a Calebite inheritance. This grounds David's earliest kingship at Hebron in the oldest layer of Judahite clan tradition: David was king at his ancestral home before being crowned at Jerusalem.
2.44: Shema's son Raham becomes "father of Jorkeam" — place-father names in Chronicles mark the genealogical origin of a town's founding clan. These "father of X" formulas preserve settlement history that might otherwise be lost: which clan first cleared, farmed, and named a particular site in Judah's hill country.
2.45: Maon son of Shammai is "father of Beth-zur" — a fortress town in the Judean hills (modern Khirbet et-Tubeiqah) that later became a significant military installation in the Maccabean period. The genealogy places its Israelite founding in the pre-monarchic Calebite settlement period, a full millennium before the Maccabees would defend it.
2.46: Ephah, Caleb's concubine, bore Haran, Moza, and Gazez. The children of concubines appear regularly in Chronicles genealogies, reflecting the legal reality that concubine-sons held clan status and land rights, if somewhat subordinate to wife-sons. Their inclusion here ensures that all Calebite branches — regardless of maternal status — are preserved in the community's memory.
2.47: Jahdai's sons — Regem, Jotham, Geshan, Pelet, Ephah, and Shaaph — form a six-name list without indication of their mother or exact relationship to Caleb. The Chronicler likely found this list in an archival source and preserves it as-is, trusting that its Calebite connection was sufficient context for post-exilic readers who held clan memory independently of written records.
2.48: Maacah, another concubine of Caleb, bore Sheber and Tirhanah. Two concubines appear in quick succession (Ephah v46, Maacah v48), suggesting Caleb's household was large and genealogically complex. The Chronicler's systematic inclusion of concubine-born lines reflects his theological conviction that all authentic Israelite lineages deserve preservation — not just the lines of "proper" wives.
2.49: Shaaph (Maacah's son) is "father of Madmannah" and "father of Machbenah/Gibea." Caleb's daughter Achsah appears here as the final named descendant — she is the same Achsah who negotiated with her father for the upper and lower springs of water as her inheritance (Josh 15:18–19; Judg 1:14–15). Her naming here without the narrative connects the genealogy's geographic claims to that earlier moment of land negotiation.
2.50: "These were the descendants of Caleb" — a formal section close. Then immediately the Chronicler reopens with a second Caleb genealogy tracing the Ephrathah line: Shobal → Kiriath-jearim; Salma → Bethlehem; Hareph → Beth-gader. These three sons of Hur anchored the three most important towns of central Judah. The genealogy is simultaneously a settlement history.
2.51: Salma as "father of Bethlehem" places David's hometown within the Calebite-Ephrathah genealogical web. Bethlehem appears later in Chronicles as the city of David's birth and the reservoir from which David's mighty men drew water at risk of their lives (1 Chr 11:17–18). Its Calebite genealogical roots ground the Davidic monarchy in ancient tribal inheritance.
2.52: Shobal as "father of Kiriath-jearim" establishes the genealogical claim to the city that hosted the ark of the covenant for twenty years (1 Sam 7:1–2). David's retrieval of the ark from Kiriath-jearim (1 Chr 13:5–6) thus becomes an act of bringing YHWH's presence from Calebite territory into Jerusalem — a territorial and theological homecoming.
2.53: The clans of Kiriath-jearim — Ithrites, Puthites, Shumathites, Mishraites — are listed as the genealogical substrate of the town. From the Mishraites descended the Zorathites and Eshtaolites. These are the same towns associated with Samson's early years (Judg 13:2, 25; 16:31), embedding the Samson narrative in a Judahite genealogical context rather than a purely Danite one.
2.54: Salma's descendants from Bethlehem include the Netophathites — whose village is mentioned in the list of returnees from Babylon (Ezra 2:22; Neh 7:26). The Chronicler's genealogy of Bethlehem thus touches both the Davidic origin and the post-exilic restoration: the same Bethlehem-rooted clans that produced David's royal line also sent families back from exile to rebuild the community. Ataroth-beth-joab may refer to a place associated with Joab, David's general.
2.55: The Kenites who settled at Jabez (a place named for the Jabez of 4:9–10) are identified as Hammath, father of the Rechabites. The Rechabites — the ascetic nomadic community who refused wine and houses and remained faithful to YHWH even when all Israel apostatized (Jer 35) — are thus given a genealogical anchor in Judah's territory. Their roots trace to Kenite metalworkers who attached themselves to Israel in the wilderness period (Judg 1:16). The Chronicler ends the Judah genealogy by including a non-Israelite clan who chose covenant loyalty above all else.

3.19: Zerubbabel, son of Pedaiah (or Shealtiel in Hag 1:1; Ezra 3:2 — a minor genealogical discrepancy, possibly explained by levirate succession), is the most significant post-exilic Davidic figure in the Chronicler’s genealogy. Historically, Zerubbabel served as the Persian-appointed governor of Yehud (the province of Judah) under Darius I (ca. 522-486 BCE), as attested in the books of Haggai (Hag 1:1: ‘Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah’) and Zechariah (Zech 4:6-10: YHWH’s word to Zerubbabel, ‘not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit’). Haggai addresses Zerubbabel as YHWH’s ḥôtām (signet ring, Hag 2:23) — a reversal of the curse on Coniah/Jehoiachin (Jer 22:24, where Coniah is torn off as a signet ring). The Chronicler’s extension of the Davidic genealogy six generations beyond Zerubbabel (into figures otherwise unknown: Hananiah, Shelomith, Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, Jushab-hesed in vv19-20; then further in vv21-24) provides a chronological anchor: six generations at 25 years per generation places the Chronicler’s composition ca. 400-350 BCE, in the late Persian period. The genealogy demonstrates that the Davidic line survived exile and continued into the Chronicler’s own era — a live covenant claim, not merely a historical record.
3.1: David's sons born at Hebron open the royal genealogy. Six sons of six different wives reflect the ANE diplomatic practice of cementing alliances through marriage: Ahinoam of Jezreel (a local Judahite alliance), Abigail the Carmelite (a prestigious match after Nabal's death), Maacah daughter of Talmai king of Geshur (a Transjordanian political alliance), Haggith/Abital/Eglah (less documented alliances or domestic wives). The international scope of David's Hebron marriages mirrors his growing political authority even before Jerusalem.
3.2: Absalom son of Maacah the Geshurite princess carries the most politically volatile genealogical combination: Davidic father + foreign royal mother. His rebellion against David (2 Samuel 15–18) may reflect the tension of a prince who felt his Geshurite royal blood entitled him to succeed without waiting for David's explicit designation. The Chronicler lists him without narrative comment, preserving the genealogical record of even the most tragic royal son.
3.3: Adonijah son of Haggith is the fourth son of Hebron; after Amnon's death and Absalom's death, he was the eldest surviving son and attempted to claim the throne before Solomon was crowned (1 Kgs 1). The Chronicler omits this narrative entirely, but the genealogical placement — fourth son, mother unknown beyond her name — subtly conveys why Adonijah believed he had a claim Solomon's supporters had to actively counter.
3.4: "Six were born to him in Hebron, where he reigned seven years and six months. And he reigned thirty-three years in Jerusalem" — the Chronicler's regnal summary for David (7.5 + 33 = 40.5 years total; cf. 2 Sam 5:4's round 40 years) grounds the genealogy in chronological history. Forty years of reign, six sons at Hebron, thirteen+ sons at Jerusalem: the multiplication of David's line is the genealogical enactment of the Abrahamic promise to multiply the patriarch's descendants.
3.5: Shimea, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon — all sons of Bathshua (Bath-Sheba) daughter of Ammiel — are listed as sons of a single mother. The Chronicler names Bathshua's father "Ammiel" (cf. 2 Sam 11:3's "Eliam" — the same person, names reversed/varied). Nathan son of David appears in Luke's genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:31) as the non-Solomonic Davidic line through which the Messiah descended on Mary's side, though this Christological significance was not available to the Chronicler.
3.6: Ibhar, Elishama, Eliphelet — these Jerusalem-born sons appear in both the Chronicles list and the Samuel parallel (2 Sam 5:15), though with some name variations. The multiplication of sons at Jerusalem reflects David's full royal establishment: multiple wives, a palace, and a stable kingdom. The Chronicler lists nine names in vv6–8 (some lists differ by 1–2) — the Jerusalem royal household at its fullest.
3.7: Nogah and Nepheg appear in Chronicles but not in the parallel 2 Sam 5 list, representing either sons omitted from the shorter Samuel account or sons born after the Samuel source was compiled. The Chronicler's longer list may draw on temple archive records that preserved a more complete royal genealogy than the court history preserved in Samuel.
3.8: Elishama (second listing), Eliada/Beeliada, and Eliphelet (second listing) — textual variants appear here compared to the Samuel parallel. "Beeliada" ("Baal knows") in 2 Sam 5:16 became "Eliada" ("God knows") in Chronicles — a deliberate substitution consistent with Chronicles' tendency to replace Baal-names with El-names. The Chronicler edits theologically even in genealogies.
3.9: "All these were David's sons, besides the sons of the concubines" — a summary note that acknowledges David's full household while preserving Tamar's name. Tamar, David's only named daughter in the genealogies, appears here without reference to her violation by Amnon (2 Samuel 13). The Chronicler's silence on that episode contrasts sharply with his inclusion of Tamar as a named individual — she is remembered as a person, not erased because of what was done to her.
3.10: Solomon → Rehoboam: the Davidic dynasty enters its sequential listing. The seventeen names from Solomon to Jeconiah/Jehoiachin (vv10–16) represent four centuries of Judahite monarchy. The Chronicler's audience knew this list not as ancient history but as their grandparents' world — the last kings before Babylon destroyed Jerusalem were within living memory of the oldest returnees.
3.11: Rehoboam → Abijah → Asa: three generations spanning the kingdom's division through the beginning of religious reform. Asa's reign marks the first sustained effort at covenant renewal after the apostasy of the divided monarchy era. The Chronicler will devote detailed narrative to Asa's reform (2 Chr 14–16) while the genealogy here notes him only as a link in the chain — the narrative and genealogy together create the full portrait.
3.12: Asa → Jehoshaphat → Joram: Jehoshaphat's alliance with the Omrides (through his son Joram's marriage to Athaliah, daughter of Ahab) is genealogically embedded here. The genealogy moves past these alliances without comment, but readers who knew the narrative (2 Chr 18–22) would hear the theological resonance: the Davidic dynasty survived even when its kings made catastrophic alliances with Israel's apostate north.
3.13: Joram → Ahaziah → Joash: Ahaziah's reign lasted one year before Jehu's coup (2 Chr 22:2–9). Joash/Jehoash was hidden in the temple for six years while Athaliah (his grandmother) ruled, then crowned at age seven (2 Chr 23). The genealogy compresses six years of temple exile into a single name-link, but the memory of that miraculous preservation — YHWH keeping the Davidic lamp burning during the deepest darkness — resonates powerfully for a community returning from Babylonian exile.
3.14: Amaziah → Azariah (Uzziah) → Jotham: the mid-monarchy period of relative stability. Uzziah's 52-year reign was the longest in Judah's history; his pride in entering the temple to burn incense (2 Chr 26:16–21) resulted in leprosy, which the genealogy entirely passes over. The genealogical format strips away narrative; only the dynastic continuity matters here.
3.15: Jotham → Ahaz → Hezekiah: the deepening crisis of the Assyrian period. Ahaz's apostasy (2 Chr 28) nearly ended the dynasty; Hezekiah's reform (2 Chr 29–32) rescued it. The single-line genealogical format places Ahaz between two reformers (Jotham and Hezekiah) without explanation — again the narrative supplements what genealogy compresses.
3.16: Hezekiah → Manasseh → Amon → Josiah: the final century of the Judahite monarchy. Manasseh's 55-year reign as the longest and initially most apostate king (2 Chr 33:1–9) ended in repentance after Assyrian captivity (2 Chr 33:10–17) — a repentance the Chronicler uniquely records. Josiah's sweeping reform (2 Chr 34–35) gave the monarchy its last great moment of covenant faithfulness before Babylon arrived. Four kings, one century, the arc of collapse and hope.
3.17: Josiah's sons — Johanan (firstborn, died before reigning), Jehoiakim (second, reigned 11 years under Babylon), Zedekiah/Mattaniah (third, last king, renamed by Nebuchadnezzar), and Shallum/Jehoahaz (fourth, reigned 3 months) — reigned in non-birth-order succession reflecting Egyptian and Babylonian imperial manipulation of Judah's throne. The Chronicler lists them in birth order while their reign order was: Jehoahaz (4th) → Jehoiakim (2nd) → Jehoiachin (Jeconiah's son) → Zedekiah (3rd).
3.18: Jeconiah/Jehoiachin's captive status is implicit in "the sons of Jeconiah, the captive" — the Hebrew word ʾāsîr (prisoner/captive) becomes his permanent epithet, encoding the Babylonian deportation of 597 BCE into the genealogical record. Yet from this captive king comes Shealtiel and the line that returns from exile. The genealogy insists: captivity does not end dynasties; YHWH's purposes continue through exile.
3.20: Hashubah, Ohel, Berechiah, Hasadiah, and Jushab-hesed — five more sons of Zerubbabel — complete the first post-exilic generation. The name Jushab-hesed ("lovingkindness/covenant-loyalty has returned") is a theological confession: the child's name declares that YHWH's covenantal faithfulness has returned to Israel after the exile. The Chronicler preserves this name as a living testimony to post-exilic hope.
3.21: Hananiah → Pelatiah and Jeshaiah; then Rephaiah's descendants; then Arnan's and Obadiah's and Shecaniah's lines. The post-exilic Davidic genealogy extends three to four generations beyond Zerubbabel, suggesting the Chronicler compiled his work sometime in the 5th–4th century BCE when these descendants were still identifiable community members. The continuation of the Davidic line, however obscure, was itself a form of messianic witness.
3.22: The sons of Shecaniah — Shemaiah and his six sons — provide the genealogy's most recent names, likely the living generation at the time of composition. These men were not kings, not governors, not even mentioned in other texts: they were ordinary members of the Davidic family living in Persian-period Yehud. The Chronicler lists them as carefully as he listed Solomon because the covenant promise had not been revoked — it simply awaited fulfillment.
3.23: Neariah's three sons and Elioenai's seven sons push the Davidic genealogy to its furthest chronological extent. Elioenai is likely one of the latest-generation names the Chronicler could verify from community records. Seven sons for the last named Davidite mirrors the seven sons of Jesse — a literary symmetry suggesting completeness, or perhaps simply reflecting the actual family size of this branch's head.
3.24: The seven sons of Elioenai — Hodaviah/Eliashib/Pelaiah/Akkub/Johanan/Delaiah/Anani — close the Davidic genealogy of Chronicles. Anani as the final name has attracted messianic interpretation in later tradition (the Aramaic word for "cloud" appears in Dan 7:13's "one like a son of man coming with the clouds"), but the Chronicler's purpose is simpler: to show that the Davidic line persisted, generation by generation, from Jesse to the present, keeping alive the covenant between YHWH and his anointed.

4.10: The prayer of Jabez (4:9-10) is one of the OT’s briefest embedded prayers, set within a genealogical list without narrative context — a single pericope preserved as a liturgical or devotional tradition. The name yaʿbēṣ (Jabez) is linked to the root ʿāṣaḇ (to pain, grieve, hurt): ‘because I bore him in pain (bᵉʿōṣeḇ).’ The prayer reverses the name-content: Jabez asks that God would ‘keep me from harm/evil (rāʿāh) so that it might not bring me pain (wᵉlōʾ yiʿaṣᵉḇênî)’ — the same verbal root. The prayer thus performs a name-reversal: the man born in pain asks to be kept from pain, and God grants it. The four-part structure (bless me, enlarge my territory, let your hand be with me, keep me from harm) reflects the Israelite petition-formula pattern attested in the Psalms of lament (petition + confidence). The geographical notation of v23 (‘those who lived among plantings and in enclosures; they lived there with the king in his service’) places a group of royal craftsmen/potters in the vicinity, possibly at Kiriath-jearim/the district of Jabez (cf. Judg 1:16), where a scribal guild is mentioned in v23. The Jabez prayer preserves, embedded in genealogy, the OT’s clearest statement that individual petition within a genealogical/tribal context is legitimate and answered: YHWH responds to specific, named persons within the covenant people, not only to the nation collectively.
4.2: The opening of 1 Chr 5 provides the Chronicler’s explanation for an anomaly in tribal history: Reuben was Jacob’s firstborn (bᵉḵôr) but did not receive the firstborn’s double-portion inheritance. The reason given is Gen 35:22 (Reuben’s violation of Bilhah, Jacob’s concubine) — the loss of the birthright is a consequence of moral failure. The Chronicler then separates two distinct forms of inheritance: the birthright (bᵉḵōrāh) goes to Joseph’s sons (= two tribes, Ephraim and Manasseh, the double portion), but the ruling-dignity (nāgîḏ) goes to Judah. This distinction between double-portion inheritance and governing leadership parallels ANE succession practices. In Mesopotamia, the firstborn typically received the family estate (aplum in Akkadian, comparable to bᵉḵōrāh), but kingship could pass to a non-firstborn son by divine designation or ability — Assyrian succession documents, particularly from the reigns of Sargon II and Esarhaddon, record the formal transfer of kingship to younger sons with theological legitimation. The Chronicler’s theological point is that the Davidic kingship (from Judah) is not an accident of birth-order but a divine allocation that the tribal system reflects: Reuben’s moral failure, Joseph’s double-portion, and Judah’s messianic designation all serve the same canonical structure.
4.1: A second Judah genealogy opens with five names — Perez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, Shobal — as if restarting from a different archival source. The Chronicler does not harmonize this with chapter 2; he juxtaposes parallel traditions, trusting readers to understand that different genealogical registers preserved overlapping information. The repetition also serves a rhetorical function: Judah's founding figures are worth naming twice.
4.3: "The sons of Etam" introduces a cluster of towns in Judah's Shephelah: Jezreel, Ishma, Idbash, and Hazzelelponi (a rare feminine name in the genealogy). These "father of" formulas — where a person's name functions as a town's founding settler — are the Chronicler's standard method for encoding settlement geography within clan genealogy. The Shephelah towns listed here formed part of the agricultural heartland of Judah before and after exile.
4.4: Penuel as "father of Gedor" and Ezer as "father of Hushah" continue the settlement-genealogy pattern. Hur, "firstborn of Ephrathah, father of Bethlehem," anchors the genealogy back to the Calebite-Ephrathah cluster of chapter 2. The repetition of Hur here creates a genealogical web rather than a simple linear descent, reflecting the complex interconnections of Judahite clan memory.
4.5: Ashhur "father of Tekoa" (mentioned first in 2:24 as Hezron's posthumous son) had two wives: Helah and Naarah. The two-wife formula generates two parallel genealogical branches from a single founding figure — a narrative device that explains why certain regions showed two distinct clan traditions tracing back to the same ancestor.
4.6: Naarah bore Ahuzzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari. These four sons of Naarah represent sub-clans of the Tekoa region. Hepher appears elsewhere as the name of a Manassite clan (Num 26:32–33) and a Canaanite city-king (Josh 12:17), reflecting the common naming phenomenon where identical names appear in different tribal contexts — either through coincidence or through clan intermarriage that spread names across tribal boundaries.
4.7: Helah bore Zereth, Izhar, and Ethnan — three more sub-clans of the Ashhur genealogy. The careful preservation of both wife-lines suggests these clans maintained distinct identities into the post-exilic period, claiming descent from different Ashhur-line ancestors as a way of differentiating their territorial claims and priestly/Levitical privileges.
4.8: Koz fathered Anub, Zobebah, and the clans of Aharhel son of Harum. The phrase "clans of Aharhel" (misphᵉḥôt ʾaḥarḥēl) indicates that Aharhel was a significant enough ancestor to have a named clan group — a misphᵉḥāh — which was the intermediate social unit between the extended family and the tribe. Such clan names were the primary administrative unit in Persian-period Yehud's demographic records.
4.9: Jabez is introduced with an unusual editorial comment: "he was more honored than his brothers." In a sea of bare names, this moral evaluation stands out. The Chronicler (or his source) felt compelled to explain why Jabez merited special treatment — and the explanation will come in v10. The contrast between his name ("pain") and his honored status sets up the prayer's power: a man born under a negative omen overcame it through direct appeal to YHWH.
4.11: Chelub, brother of Shuhah, fathered Mehir who was "father of Eshton." The Chelubai/Caleb names that recur in these Judahite genealogies reflect either the fame of the Caleb tradition (causing multiple unrelated families to claim Calebite descent) or genuine genealogical branching where Caleb's name was common enough to generate confusion across generations.
4.12: Eshton fathered Beth-rapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah — Tehinnah being the "father of Ir-nahash." "Ir-nahash" means "city of the serpent/copper," possibly associated with copper-smelting activities in the Arabah region south of the Dead Sea. The Kenite metalworking connections visible in chapter 2 (v55) resurface here: Judah's southern clans maintained craft traditions involving metalwork that shaped their geographic and economic identity.
4.13: Kenaz's sons Othniel and Seraiah connect the Calebite genealogy to the judge Othniel son of Kenaz, Caleb's younger brother (Judg 3:9–11). Othniel, the first judge of Israel and a Calebite hero, is here placed within the broader Judahite genealogical web. His inclusion affirms that Judah's covenant story included not just kings but deliverers — the same YHWH who raised up Othniel raised up David.
4.14: Meonothai fathered Ophrah; Seraiah fathered Joab who was "father of Ge-harashim" — "Valley of Craftsmen." This Joab (not David's general) was the founding ancestor of a craftsmen's guild town in Judah. The notice that they were craftsmen (ḥārāšîm) embedded a professional identity within the genealogical record — these families preserved their vocational heritage through the exile, returning not just as generic Israelites but as skilled artisans with a specific role in rebuilding.
4.15: Caleb son of Jephunneh (the spy, distinct from Caleb son of Hezron) had sons Iru, Elah, and Naam. Elah's son Kenaz connects to the broader Kenazite clan tradition. Caleb son of Jephunneh's inclusion in the Judahite genealogy confirms his full tribal membership despite his Kenazite background — the spy who trusted YHWH when all others faltered was fully incorporated into Judah's genealogical heritage.
4.16: Jehallelel's four sons — Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, Asarel — anchor the Ziph wilderness region within Judah's genealogy. Ziph is significant as David's hiding place during the Saul period (1 Sam 23:14–24; 26:1), and the Ziphite betrayal of David is the narrative dark side of this genealogical connection. The descendants of Ziph's founding ancestor were the same people who twice reported David's location to Saul.
4.17: Ezra's sons include Jether, Mered, Epher, and Jalon; Mered's wife Bithiah (or Bityah) is identified as Pharaoh's daughter. A daughter of Pharaoh marrying into Judah's genealogy — and the Chronicler preserves her Egyptian royal title without embarrassment — reinforces the inclusive genealogical vision of these chapters. Bithiah/Bityah means "daughter of YHWH," a Hebrew name that the Egyptian princess apparently adopted upon entering the covenant community.
4.18: Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh bore Miriam, Shammai, and Ishbah (father of Eshtemoa). Eshtemoa is the Levitical city in the Judean hills where David distributed plunder after the Ziklag raid (1 Sam 30:28). That its founding ancestor was born of an Egyptian princess further illustrates the mixed cultural origins of Judah's settlements — origins the Chronicler preserves as legitimate rather than embarrassing.
4.19: Sons of Hodiah's wife (sister of Naham): the father of Keilah the Garmite and Eshtemoa the Maacathite. Keilah is the town David rescued from Philistines (1 Sam 23:1–13) — only to discover its citizens would hand him over to Saul if Saul came. The genealogy of Keilah's founding ancestor sits between genealogical lists of Judahite towns, mapping David's entire wilderness-exile geography onto ancestral territory.
4.20: Shimon's sons and Ishi's sons — including Zoheth and Ben-zoheth — continue the dense southern Judahite genealogy. The multiplication of names without narrative commentary characterizes this section of Chronicles as a documentary archive: names preserved not for their stories but for their genealogical function as clan-anchors for post-exilic families claiming Judahite land rights.
4.21: The sons of Shelah son of Judah (Judah's third son by the Canaanite Bathshua) founded linen-working guilds: Er father of Lecah, Laadah father of Mareshah, and the families of the linen workers at Beth-ashbea. The preservation of craft guild genealogies alongside tribal genealogies reflects the Chronicler's understanding that YHWH's purposes were served by skilled workers as surely as by warriors and priests.
4.22: Jokim, men of Cozeba, Joash, and Saraph — who ruled in Moab and returned to Lehem — with the note that "the records are ancient." The Chronicler's acknowledgment that his sources are old is a rare moment of historiographical transparency: he knows he is working from fragmentary materials and signals this explicitly rather than pretending to complete knowledge. The Moabite connection in Shelah's line echoes the Ruth narrative's Moabite strand within Judah's story.
4.23: "These were the potters who lived at Netaim and Gederah; they lived there in the king's service." The genealogy closes with a craftsmen's note: the Shelah clans were royal potters, producing vessels for the court and temple. This royal service connection — *ʿim-hammelek bᵉmlāʾktô* — preserved the clan's identity as skilled artisans in royal employ. Post-exilic readers who had returned to rebuild the temple would have recognized their own craft-heritage ancestors in this notice.
4.24: Simeon's genealogy opens with five sons: Nemuel/Jemuel, Jamin, Jarib/Jachin, Zerah/Zohar, and Shaul. The variations from Genesis 46:10 reflect divergent traditions — the Chronicler notes them without harmonizing. Simeon's territory was embedded within Judah's (Josh 19:1–9) because Simeon's inheritance was insufficient for a full tribal settlement; genealogically and geographically, Simeon was absorbed into Judah during the monarchic period.
4.25: Simeon's genealogy continues: Shallum → Mibsam → Mishma. These three-generation descents track Simeonite clan development from the wilderness period into the settlement era. The compressed format — three names with no narrative — reflects how Simeon's tribal history faded from prominence once the tribe lost independent territorial status within Judah.
4.26: Mishma's sons: Hammuel → Zaccur → Shimei with his sixteen sons and six daughters. The Chronicler notes Shimei's large family as unusual — "but his brothers did not have many children, so their whole clan did not multiply as much as Judah." This demographic comparison acknowledges Simeon's relative smallness within the larger Judahite context, explaining the tribe's eventual absorption without attributing blame.
4.27: "Simeon's sons did not multiply as much as the men of Judah" — a rare demographic observation that frames Simeon's genealogy as a story of diminishment rather than growth. The reason given is simply that they had fewer children — no theological explanation is offered. The Chronicler's restraint here is notable: not every tribal diminishment requires a moral explanation; sometimes demography is simply demography.
4.28: Simeon's settlements: Beer-sheba, Moladah, Hazar-shual, and others listed in vv28–31 correspond closely to Joshua 19:1–8. Beer-sheba, the southernmost Judahite city and the endpoint of the "Dan to Beersheba" idiom, was a Simeonite settlement before becoming a generic symbol for Israel's geographic boundaries. The genealogy embeds this geographic knowledge within Simeonite clan memory.
4.29: The continuation of Simeonite settlements: Bilhah, Ezem, Tolad, Bethuel, Hormah, and Ziklag. Ziklag — the Philistine city given to David by Achish (1 Sam 27:6) — is here identified as a Simeonite settlement, explaining why an Israelite city would have ended up in Philistine control. The post-exilic reader would have understood: Simeon's territorial vacuum in the south created the conditions for Philistine encroachment.
4.30: Beth-marcaboth, Hazar-susim, Beth-biri, and Shaaraim — with the note that "these were their cities until David became king." The temporal marker "until David" indicates a significant territorial reorganization: David's kingdom absorbed Simeonite settlements into the royal administrative network, ending their status as distinctly Simeonite cities. The Chronicler preserves the pre-Davidic settlement list as historical record.
4.31: The five additional settlements and the genealogical note that these were Simeon's cities close the geographic section. The combination of genealogy and settlement list creates a territorial claim: post-exilic Simeonites (if any remained as a distinct group) could point to this list as evidence of their ancestral claim to southern Judean towns. For the Chronicler, preserving these lists was an act of justice toward smaller, less prominent tribal groups.
4.32: Thirteen leaders of Simeonite clans are named as "princes in their clans" — a title reflecting the Persian-period administrative structure where clan leaders (nᵉśîʾîm) held recognized authority over family groups. The Chronicler's listing of these names suggests he had access to relatively contemporary Simeonite clan records, not just ancient genealogies.
4.33: The summary: these Simeonite families multiplied greatly and went to Gerar as far as the eastern side of the valley, seeking pasture. The expansion movement reflects Simeon's agro-pastoral economy: when the traditional territory became overpopulated, clans expanded outward into marginal grazing land. This pastoral pressure-valve migration is a recurring pattern in ancient Near Eastern tribal history.
4.34: Simeonite princes named here — Meshobab, Jamlech, Joshah, Joel, Jehu, Elioenai, Jaakobah, Jeshohaiah, Asaiah, Adiel, Jesimiel, Benaiah, Ziza — form an unusually long list of named leaders. The name density suggests the Chronicler had access to a Simeonite administrative document, perhaps from the Persian-period satrapy records, rather than ancient tribal memory alone.
4.35: Elioenai and others descended from "Shihi" continue the Simeonite leadership register. The names become less recognizable as they approach the Chronicler's own time — suggesting these were contemporary figures whose records were still accessible in Persian-period archives rather than names reconstructed from ancient oral tradition.
4.36: The continuation of Simeonite prince names through Jeshaiah and Adiel closes the leadership register. These thirteen men (or their clans) represent the organizational structure of Simeon's community within Persian-period Yehud — small enough to lose independent tribal identity but large enough to maintain a genealogical record that the Chronicler deemed worth preserving.
4.37: Ziza son of Shiphi through Allon to Jedaiah — a four-generation descent ending in one of the named Simeonite expansion leaders. The genealogical depth here (four generations from a named ancestor) suggests this was a recent enough event — the Hezekiah-era expansion to Gedor — that four generations of ancestry could still be verified from family memory or clan records.
4.38: "These mentioned by name were princes in their clans, and their fathers' houses increased greatly." The summary before the expansion narrative affirms that genealogical record-keeping and territorial expansion went together: families grew, needed land, and used their genealogical credentials to establish claims to new territory. The Chronicler presents this as legitimate and divinely providential.
4.39: The Simeonites sought pasture for their flocks at Gedor (possibly Gerar, the Philistine territory) near the eastern valley. The peaceful pastoral expansion narrative — seeking grazing land rather than waging war — reflects the Chronicler's understanding that post-exilic community growth happened through diligence and opportunity rather than conquest. The same model applied to the returning exiles: find good land, establish claims, build.
4.40: The land was spacious, quiet, and peaceful — fat pasture. The description echoes Canaan's original appeal to the patriarchs and the pre-conquest spies' reports. "The former inhabitants were Hamites" — the Chronicler identifies the pre-Israelite population, perhaps to explain the vacancy or to establish that the Simeonites were displacing a non-Israelite population rather than competing with fellow Israelites.
4.41: The Simeonites under Hezekiah destroyed the Hamite and Meunite settlements and settled in their place. The note that this happened "in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah" dates the expansion to the late 8th century BCE, during the same period when Hezekiah was conducting religious reform (2 Chr 29–31). Territorial expansion and covenant renewal went together in the Chronicler's historiography.
4.42: Five hundred Simeonites went further to Mount Seir, led by Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel — sons of Ishi. They struck the remaining Amalekites and "lived there to this day." The Chronicler's use of "to this day" indicates either a source document that used this phrase contemporaneously, or that some Simeonite presence in the Seir region persisted into his own era. The destruction of Amalekites resonates with the command of 1 Sam 15 — Simeon here completes what Saul failed to do.
4.43: "They struck the remnant of the Amalekites who had escaped, and they have lived there to this day." The Amalekites — Israel's archetypal enemy since Exodus 17 — are finally eliminated by an otherwise obscure Simeonite expedition during Hezekiah's reign. The Chronicler places this fulfillment of ancient covenant mandate within a genealogical context: even the smallest tribal groups, when faithful, could accomplish what the great king Saul could not.

5.6: The deportation of Beerah the Reubenite chief by Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria — ʾōtô hᵉglāh tilgat pilneser melek ʾaššûr, ‘him Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria carried into exile’ — is confirmed by Assyrian royal records. Tiglath-pileser III (744-727 BCE), known in Babylonian sources as Pul (cf. 5:26 where the Chronicler equates Pul and Tiglath-pileser), conducted systematic deportation campaigns in the Transjordanian territories during 734-732 BCE. His annals and the Iran Stele record the deportation of populations from ‘the wide land of Bit-Humria’ (the house of Omri = Israel/Samaria) and surrounding regions; 2 Kgs 15:29 records the same Transjordanian deportation.
